Thought for 9s


You already have a DIR_ID concept in SSOT: a two-layer directory identity (physical .dir_id + semantic keys) with deterministic hashing and enforcement gates. Model the “dir id system” after the file id system by giving it the same contract-driven ingest + schema + write-policy + evidence + gates shape, while keeping directory IDs hash-derived (not counter-allocated) to avoid rename/move ambiguity.

Target behavior (mirrors File ID system structure)



A. Directory Identity = 2 layers (same pattern as SSOT)


Layer 1: Physical identity (dir_id) stored in a .dir_id file in each governed directory; deterministic from repo-relative path via slugify + blake2b (8 hex).
DOC-GUIDE-DIR-ID-IMPLEMENTATION…

Layer 2: Stable semantic identity (semantic_key) stored centrally (e.g., config/path_index.yaml) so scripts can reference directories by meaning even after refactors; the index updates on moves.
DOC-GUIDE-DIR-ID-IMPLEMENTATION…


This is directly analogous to:


file: file_id + “where it lives” (repo_root_id, relative_path)

dir: dir_id + “where it lives” (repo_root_id, relative_path) + stable meaning (semantic_key).


B. Deterministic ingest workflow (clone your file-ingest phases)


Use the same “contracts decide headers + order + population” approach from the ingest spec, but with directory-specific steps.

One-page “DIR_ID System Model” (what another AI should implement)



Inputs


observed path event (filesystem watcher)

repo_root map

registry snapshot

contracts (dictionary/write-policy/derivations/schema)

path_index (semantic map)


Outputs


.dir_id file created/updated (if governed)

registry patch adds/updates directory entity record

evidence artifacts: scan event, computed hash inputs, versions, before/after snapshots


Determinism invariants


dir_id is pure function of normalized relative_path under pinned algorithm/version.

all writes are single-writer atomic commits.

header selection and field derivation are contract-driven (not hardcoded).
INGEST_SPEC_UNIFIED_REGISTRY



If you want this “dir id system model” to be exactly parallel to your file id system at runtime, the critical move is: make directories ingest through the same contract pipeline (same style as the ingest spec), but with hash-derived identity and .dir_id as the directory-side equivalent of an ID-prefixed filename.


INGEST_SPEC_UNIFIED_REGISTRY


DOC-GUIDE-DIR-ID-IMPLEMENTATION…

Objective


Make directory identity flow through the exact same runtime pipeline as file identity: same contracts, same phases, same patch discipline, same evidence, same gates. The only difference: identity source (files may be allocated; directories are hash-derived) and where the ID is stored (.dir_id instead of an ID-prefixed filename).



Mirror the file runtime pipeline phases, 1:1



Phase A — Discovery (same trigger model)


File pipeline: file watcher / scan yields relative_path, stat, maybe content hash.
Dir pipeline: directory watcher / scan yields relative_path, stat, child counts.


Output (both): a normalized “ingest candidate” record:


repo_root_id

relative_path (canonical separators)

entity_kind = file or directory

basic stat metadata


Phase B — Contract Load (identical for both)


Load the same four governance contracts, just with directory predicates applying:


Column Dictionary (header definitions + required_when rules)

Derivations policy (how fields are computed)

Write policy (what is allowed to be written/overwritten, patch-only rules)

Registry schema (valid shape)


Critical rule: runtime code never “decides” fields; contracts decide.


Phase C — Header Selection (identical mechanism)


Compute column_headers for the entity by evaluating required_when predicates.


For files: predicates like is_python, artifact_kind=doc, etc.

For directories: predicates like entity_kind=directory, dir_governed=true, scope=repo, etc.


Result: deterministic set/order of fields that must be populated.


Phase D — Identity Resolution (parallel structure, different source)


This is where you make directories parallel without copying file logic incorrectly.


File identity resolution (typical):


If filename already contains file_id → parse/validate → use

Else allocate/assign file_id → rename or store mapping (depending on your model)


Directory identity resolution (parallel):


If .dir_id exists → read/validate → use

Else compute hash-derived dir_id from normalized relative_path → write .dir_id


Parallelism point: both are “identity resolution” subroutines with the same interface:


input: repo_root_id, relative_path, contracts, existing_artifacts

output: id_value, id_source, id_validation_result, mutations_required


Phase E — Field Population (same engine)


Populate fields in this order (same for file + dir):


Identity fields (file_id or dir_id, scope fields)

Direct observation fields (stats, timestamps, path)

Derived fields (based on derivations contract)

Evidence pointers (what evidence artifacts were produced)


No ad hoc exceptions: directory fields are just another entity profile.


Phase F — Validation Gates (same concept, dir-specific gate catalog)


Run the same gate runner framework, just with a directory gate set:


schema validation

required fields present

identity validation

write-policy compliance

collision and uniqueness checks


Phase G — Commit (same patch-only discipline)


Registry mutation must be patch-only:


add/update the directory record via RFC-6902 patch

update/append evidence manifest

optionally update semantic index (also patch-only)


Phase H — Post-run verification (same pattern)


confirm .dir_id exists and matches expected value

confirm registry record matches schema + required headers

confirm no illegal writes happened



Make .dir_id the directory-side equivalent of an ID-prefixed filename



To be a true parallel, .dir_id must behave like “the thing you can look at locally to recover identity”.


Operational rules:


Every governed directory contains exactly one .dir_id file at its root.

.dir_id contains:
dir_id

algo_version

normalized_relative_path used for hashing

repo_root_id (optional but useful for portability)

.dir_id is treated as authoritative local identity the same way an ID-prefixed filename is for files.


Key property: you can reconstruct the registry record for a directory from the filesystem alone, same as files.



Define directory identity as hash-derived, version-pinned



To keep runtime deterministic and parallel-friendly:


dir_id = DIR-<slug>-<hash8>

hash input = normalized repo-relative path

algorithm/version pinned in contract (not code)


Why this matters for “exact parallel”:


File identity has a pinned allocation/parsing rule.

Directory identity must have a pinned derivation rule.

Both are contract-governed identity rules.



Make directory entities first-class registry records



Parallel runtime requires the registry to treat directories as real entities, not “metadata about paths”.


Directory record should include (minimum):


entity_kind: directory

repo_root_id

relative_path

dir_id

dir_id_source: computed|existing|migrated

governance_scope / dir_governed

timestamps (allocated_at, observed_at, etc.)

evidence pointers


This allows the exact same registry writer, validator, and gate runner to process directories and files.



Evidence: require the same evidence envelope, different payload



If file ingest produces evidence like:


“hash inputs”, “file hash”, “parse results”, “gate report”


Then dir ingest must produce:


“hash inputs” (path normalization inputs)

.dir_id write (before/after or write receipt)

“directory snapshot” (counts, immediate children list hash)

“gate report”


Important: Evidence types should be defined in the same evidence catalog; just add directory-specific evidence kinds.



Semantic keys are optional but must follow the same pipeline



If you maintain a path_index / semantic map:


treat semantic updates as another entity record mutation governed by write-policy

patch-only updates

gate it (coverage thresholds, uniqueness, no broken references)


That keeps “directory meaning” parallel to “file meaning” (e.g., doc_id mappings, artifact roles).



Runtime “how-to” checklist (implementation-spec level, no code)



Add entity_kind=directory handling to the same ingest entrypoint.

Ensure contract loader is shared (no dir-only hardcoding).

Extend header selection predicates for directory headers.

Implement resolve_directory_identity() as the exact sibling of resolve_file_identity():
check local identity artifact

validate

otherwise compute and write

Ensure registry writer accepts directory records and produces RFC-6902 patches.

Add directory gates to the same gate runner.

Add directory evidence artifacts to the same evidence manifest system.

Add post-run verification for .dir_id and registry consistency.



What “exactly parallel at runtime” looks like in one sentence



A directory goes through the same ingest pipeline states as a file (discover → contract → headers → identity → populate → gate → patch → verify), with .dir_id serving the same “local identity anchor” role that an ID-prefixed filename provides for files, and with identity computed deterministically from normalized path under a pinned algorithm.


If you want, I can express this as a strict, machine-enforceable runtime contract (JSON) that defines: phases, required inputs/outputs per phase, gating rules, and required evidence artifacts (still no code).

Thought for a couple of seconds


No. My earlier response implied “same pipeline” but it did not explicitly separate trigger events enough. Here is the crisp separation you need so another AI can implement it without mixing semantics.


Trigger events are different; the pipeline is shared


File ID system triggers (examples)


A file record enters the ingest pipeline when any of these happen:


FILE_CREATED (new file appears)

FILE_MODIFIED (content or metadata changes)

FILE_MOVED/RENAMED (path changes)

FILE_DELETED (tombstone / registry update)

BATCH_SCAN_FILE_FOUND (discovered during scan, not watcher)


Dir ID system triggers (the one you care about)


A directory record enters the ingest pipeline when any of these happen:


DIR_CREATED (new folder appears) ✅ primary

DIR_MOVED/RENAMED (path changes ⇒ may require recompute or alias handling)

DIR_DELETED (tombstone / registry update)

BATCH_SCAN_DIR_FOUND (discovered during scan)


Key point: the event source differs (file watcher vs directory watcher), but both feed the same contract ingest pipeline with entity_kind = file|directory.


Make the difference unmissable in the runtime contract


To make it deterministic and unambiguous, define a single “ingest envelope” that always includes:


trigger_event_kind (one of the above)

entity_kind (file or directory)

relative_path

repo_root_id

observed_at


Then enforce these two invariants:


Routing invariant
If trigger_event_kind starts with DIR_* ⇒ entity_kind MUST be directory


If trigger_event_kind starts with FILE_* ⇒ entity_kind MUST be file
Identity-resolution invariant


If entity_kind=directory ⇒ identity source is hash-derived and stored in .dir_id

If entity_kind=file ⇒ identity source is file_id resolution (parse/allocate per your file rules)


Minimal “how it looks” state machine


Event (DIR_CREATED)
→ IngestCandidate (entity_kind=directory)
→ ContractLoad
→ HeaderSelection (directory predicates)
→ IdentityResolution (read .dir_id else compute+write)
→ Populate
→ Gates (directory gate set)
→ PatchCommit (registry)
→ Verify


That makes the trigger difference explicit while keeping runtime exactly parallel from “IngestCandidate” onward.


If you want this to be impossible to misread, the next step is to write a strict JSON runtime contract section named trigger_matrix mapping {trigger_event_kind -> entity_kind -> required_subpipeline} and gate it (fail fast if mismatched).

Thought for 15s


What you’re asking for (in system terms)


You want one portable “ID package” that you can drop onto any project root, where:


Files and directories share the same identity runtime (same contracts, same registry writer, same gates, same evidence model).

IDs never collide across projects, because the ID counter is global/persistent and does not reset per project.

The project root acts like a staging zone:
root-level loose files/folders can exist without IDs

but everything below root (depth ≥ 1) is governed:
every directory must have a directory ID

every file must have a file ID

Temporary/reference files should not clutter the registry:
keep them in root staging or in explicitly excluded paths

When a file without an ID is “added to the registry,” the system:
generates an ID value

renames the file to prefix the ID

for Python files specifically: P_ is added in front of the file ID in the filename


Below is the clean way to structure this so it’s deterministic, portable, and not two separate systems.



Treat “ID Package” as a single subsystem with three parts



A) Global ID allocator (shared by files + dirs)


One allocator issues the “next ID” for either entity type.

It is the only stateful part that must persist across projects.


B) Project scope policy (what requires IDs where)


A deterministic rule for “root staging vs governed subtree.”


C) Ingest pipeline (same pipeline for both entity kinds)


Discovery → contracts → header selection → identity resolution → populate → gate → patch/commit → verify

entity_kind switches behavior only inside identity resolution and required fields.


This makes file IDs and directory IDs a single operating package.



Global ID allocator: how to prevent cross-project collisions



You need a global, portable, persistent counter that is NOT stored inside each project root.


Required properties


Single source of truth for “next ID”

Atomic allocation (no duplicates even if two processes allocate)

Portable across projects

Auditable (who allocated what, when, for what path)


Concrete structure (recommended)


Global Allocator Store (outside any project), e.g.:


~/.gov_reg/id_allocator/
allocator_state.json (contains last_issued_id, allocator_version)

allocation_log.ndjson (append-only log of allocations)


Allocation record (minimum)


issued_id

issued_at_utc

entity_kind (file|directory)

project_root_id (stable identifier for the project root)

relative_path_at_allocation (where it was when allocated)

reason (register|promote|mkdir_governed|import)

tool_version


This is what makes the package safely reusable: you move the package, not the counter reset.

If you want the counter to follow you across machines too, the allocator store must live in a synced location (or a central repo). Same structure, different storage.


Project root policy: staging vs governed subtree



Define one rule and enforce it everywhere:


Depth rule


Let project_root be user-specified.

Define depth = number of path segments below project_root.

Depth 0 (root): STAGING
IDs optional

registry optional

can contain temp/reference files/folders

Depth ≥ 1: GOVERNED
every directory must have a directory ID

every file must have a file ID

creation/move into governed area triggers enforcement


Why this works for your workflow


You can prototype in root without paying governance cost.

Anything you “promote” into the real project structure gets IDs automatically and consistently.

The registry stays clean.



Enforcement model: promotion is the bridge



You need an explicit concept:


“Promotion” = moving from staging → governed


When something crosses the boundary (depth 0 → depth ≥ 1), the system enforces:


directory ID present (or created)

file IDs present (or created + renamed)

registry entries created/updated

deterministic derived columns populated per contract


This is the mechanism that keeps “temp work” from polluting the registry while still letting you use the system reliably.



Directory IDs: keep .dir_id, but base it on the same allocator ID space



You said the counter determines the next ID number for either file or directory. That means directory IDs should be allocated (not path-hash-derived) if you want strict “one counter for everything.”


Directory identity artifact


Each governed directory contains a .dir_id file (local anchor).

.dir_id contents include:
dir_id (allocated from global allocator)

project_root_id

allocated_at_utc

allocator_version

optional: created_by, notes


This keeps directory identity stable across renames/moves and matches your “shared counter” requirement.



File IDs: allocation + filename prefixing (with Python rule)



File ID allocation


When a file is registered/promoted into governed space:
allocate file_id from the same allocator (shared counter)

store file_id in registry

rename the file to prefix the ID


Filename prefix rules


You need a deterministic filename scheme. Example structure:


Non-Python:
<FILE_ID>__<original_name>

Python:
P_<FILE_ID>__<original_name>.py


Registry fields you’ll want to keep deterministic


file_id (pure identity)

filename_current

filename_original

id_prefix_applied (bool)

id_prefix_scheme_version


This preserves the original human name while still enforcing the ID in the filename.



“Register without ID” behavior (your requirement)



You said: “files without ID when added to the register, generate ID only as a separate value, but prefixed to the front of the file name…”


That implies registration triggers rename.


So define two states:


A) Unregistered (allowed in staging)


no ID

no registry row (or optionally “observed_only”, but you explicitly don’t want clutter)


B) Registered (required in governed)


has ID

in registry

filename prefixed (and Python gets P_)


The act of registering/promoting is when IDs are created and the rename happens.



One unified trigger model (so file+dir aren’t “separate systems”)



They can have different event sources but must produce the same ingest envelope:


Trigger sources


DIR_CREATED / DIR_MOVED into governed zone

FILE_CREATED / FILE_MOVED into governed zone

manual “PROMOTE” command (staging → governed)


Single ingest envelope (required fields)


trigger_event_kind

entity_kind

project_root

relative_path

depth

governance_zone (staging|governed)

desired_action (ignore|register|promote|update|tombstone)


Routing is deterministic:


if zone=staging → do nothing unless explicit register/promote

if zone=governed → enforce IDs + registry + derived columns


This makes file+dir identity a single runtime package, not two programs.



What you should hard-spec as “laws” (non-negotiable invariants)



Global allocator is authoritative for uniqueness across projects.

Governed zone is depth ≥ 1 below project root.

Every governed directory has .dir_id.

Every governed file has an ID and filename prefix; Python adds P_.

Staging zone does not require IDs and is excluded from registry by default.

Crossing staging→governed triggers promotion enforcement.

Registry population and derived columns are contract-driven and deterministic.



What you still need to decide (but you don’t need to “split systems”)



These are policy choices, not architectural splits:


Excluded subtrees inside governed (e.g., build/, .venv/, __pycache__/)
If yes, define an exclusion_policy list.

What happens on move/rename
For directories: .dir_id stays the same.

For files: file_id stays the same; filename prefix stays; only the “human name” portion may change.

Does registry include staging at all?
Strong recommendation given your goal: default no, except an optional minimal “staging inventory” if you ever want observability.


If you want, I can rewrite everything above as a strict machine-enforceable spec (JSON) with:


allocator contract

project root policy contract

promotion contract

filename scheme contract (including Python P_)

gate catalog for enforcement

registry write rules and required derived fields

Yes — it is helpful to AI, but only if you do it selectively and formally, not as a blanket rule.


Below is the straight answer, with a concrete policy that fits your system and avoids unnecessary complexity.



Short answer


Python needs P_ → this is justified and should stay.

Not all file types should get a prefix → that would add noise.

Some high-impact categories benefit from a prefix if (and only if) they have distinct lifecycle, tooling, or execution semantics.


The key is: prefixes should encode execution semantics, not file extensions.



Why P_ is genuinely useful (and different)


Python files are special in your system because:


They are executable

They are parsed, analyzed, imported, linted, and dependency-checked

They often act as tools, validators, or orchestrators

They have filename constraints (import, module resolution)


So P_<FILE_ID>__name.py gives AI immediate semantic signal:

“This file participates in runtime behavior and tooling.”

That is real value — not cosmetic.



When additional prefixes help AI (criteria)


A prefix helps only if ALL of these are true:


The file type has distinct governance rules

It participates in different pipelines or gates

It has different lifecycle expectations

AI decisions change based on knowing this immediately from the filename


If not, the prefix is redundant because:


the registry already knows the type

the extension already signals it

the prefix adds parsing overhead without decision value



Recommended prefix taxonomy (minimal, high-signal)


✅ KEEP

Prefix
Meaning
Why it helps
P_
Python executable / module
Execution, imports, tooling, AST, runtime
(none)
Generic artifacts
Let extension + registry handle it

⚠️ OPTIONAL (only if you enforce distinct rules)


Add only if you actually enforce different behavior.

Prefix
Use case
When it’s worth it
S_
Schemas (JSON/YAML schema files)
If schemas have special validation + versioning
C_
Contracts / governance law
If contracts are immutable / gated differently
D_
Docs (authoritative docs)
Only if docs have special registry rules
T_
Tests
Only if test discovery is filename-driven

If you don’t enforce different gates, don’t add the prefix.



What you should NOT do


❌ Prefix everything just to be consistent
❌ Prefix based on extension alone
❌ Prefix low-value artifacts (images, notes, scratch files)
❌ Create dozens of prefixes AI has to memorize


That would hurt AI performance, not help it.



Deterministic rule AI can follow (important)


AI must never guess prefixes.


Define this law:

A file gets a letter prefix if and only if it belongs to a file class with a distinct execution or governance contract.

Everything else:


ID prefix only

Extension + registry provide semantics



How this fits your ID system cleanly


Filename structure stays consistent:


<PREFIX_?><FILE_ID>__<human_name>.<ext>


Examples:


P_01999000042260124506__validator.py

C_01999000042260124510__registry_write_policy.yaml

01999000042260124522__notes.md


This keeps:


ID parsing trivial

prefix optional

AI reasoning deterministic



Final recommendation


Yes, prefixes help AI — but only when they encode real behavioral meaning.

Python is the strongest and clearest case.

Add others only if they map to different gates, lifecycle rules, or execution paths.

Otherwise, rely on the registry and extensions — that’s what they’re for.


If you want, next step would be:


a formal prefix policy table (machine-enforceable)

a gate that rejects illegal prefix/type combinations

a migration rule for adding prefixes without breaking history

Yes, but only a few are worth adding. Anything beyond that becomes noise unless you actually gate/enforce different behavior.


Prefixes worth considering beyond the “minimal” set


Execution and automation


M_ = MQL4 / MT4 scripts (if you govern trading code separately)

X_ = executable scripts (PowerShell/Bash) when they are runtime tools (if you don’t want to rely on .ps1 / .sh)

A_ = automation/orchestrator entrypoints (if you have “run these” vs “library code” distinction)


Governance and determinism (high value in your framework)


G_ = gate definitions / gate catalogs (files that define gating rules)

E_ = evidence artifacts (generated outputs that should never be hand-edited)

R_ = registries (SSOT registries, indexes, inventories) if you want quick filename-level recognition


Planning / execution contracts


L_ = laws (hard governance law; immutable baseline)

K_ = contracts (formal machine-enforceable specs; may overlap with C_—pick one and stick to it)

W_ = work plans / runbooks (plans intended to be executed by the CLI)


Data and config


F_ = fixtures (golden test fixtures, deterministic baseline inputs)

N_ = normalized datasets (canonicalized, used for comparison/determinism)

Z_ = configs (only if configs are treated specially vs general YAML/JSON)


The hard rule (or this will collapse)


Add a prefix only if it drives at least one of these:


different gates

different write policy (e.g., “generated, do not edit”)

different execution semantics (“entrypoint” vs “library”)

different lifecycle (“baseline” vs “patch” vs “evidence”)


If it doesn’t change behavior, don’t add it.


Keep the prefix set small


Recommended practical ceiling: 8–12 total prefixes, including P_. Beyond that, AI and humans start making mistakes.


Suggested “expanded but still sane” set for your system


If I had to pick an expanded set aligned to your deterministic governance workflow:


P_ Python

X_ executable script entrypoints (ps1/sh)

R_ registries

K_ contracts (or keep C_—choose one)

G_ gate catalogs/validators

E_ evidence outputs (generated)

F_ fixtures (golden baselines)

W_ executable plans/runbooks

S_ schemas


That’s 9 prefixes. Anything else should be justified by actual enforcement rules.


If you want this to be machine-enforceable, the next step is to define a prefix_policy mapping: {prefix -> allowed_extensions -> artifact_kind -> write_policy_class -> required_gates} so the CLI can validate it deterministically.

Thought for 24s


According to the project files, you already have several concrete mechanisms that add useful detail to the approach (gates, CI, exclusion rules, conflict test vectors, prefix scanners, allocator locking). The biggest gap is that the current DIR_ID spec is hash-derived, while your desired “one package / one counter across files+dirs” implies allocator-issued IDs for directories. You can still reuse most of the framework, but you must explicitly choose which model wins.


Useful additions the project files already define



A) Directory ID gates + CI wiring (already specified)


The DIR_ID guide defines a gate suite and shows how to run it locally and in GitHub Actions:


Gate G: validate .dir_id files (existence, format, path match, hash match)

Gate H: hardcoded path detection (enforced)

Gate I: semantic key coverage (coverage % and thresholds)

Example GitHub Actions workflow to run these gates on push/PR

DOC-GUIDE-DIR-ID-IMPLEMENTATION…


How this improves your approach: it gives you a ready-made enforcement layer so dir IDs are not “best effort”; they’re blocking.

ID Package Policy v1.0


Scope: This policy defines one unified, portable identity system (“ID Package”) that governs both directories and files under a user-specified project root, while allowing a root-level staging zone to avoid registry clutter from temporary artifacts.



Core Principles


1.1 Single Package, Single Runtime


Files and directories are governed by one shared ingest/runtime framework (same contract pipeline, same validation gates, same patch discipline, same evidence patterns).

Differences between files and directories are expressed only via entity_kind and identity_artifact_type, not via separate subsystems.


1.2 Global Uniqueness Across Projects


All IDs (file IDs and directory IDs) are drawn from one global ID allocator that persists outside any specific project.

The allocator MUST guarantee no ID duplicates across:
different projects

different machines (if allocator state is shared/synced)

concurrent runs (atomic allocation)


1.3 Staging vs Governed


The project root is treated as staging (low-governance) by default.

Anything below the project root (depth ≥ 1) is governed (full identity + registry + determinism).



Definitions


2.1 Project Root


project_root_path: User-supplied directory path that defines the boundary for governance.


2.2 Depth


depth = number of path segments below project_root_path
root itself is depth = 0

immediate children are depth = 1


2.3 Governance Zones


zone = staging iff depth = 0

zone = governed iff depth >= 1

(Optional) zone = excluded if path matches explicit exclusion policy.



Identity Model


3.1 Single Global ID Space


Both file_id and dir_id are allocated from the same ID sequence.

IDs are never derived from path hashes for governed identity.


3.2 Directory Identity


3.2.1 Directory Identity Artifact


Every governed directory MUST contain a .dir_id file in its directory root.

.dir_id is the authoritative local anchor for directory identity.


3.2.2 Directory ID Assignment Rule


If directory is in zone=governed:
If .dir_id exists and is valid → use its dir_id

Else allocate a new dir_id from the global allocator and write .dir_id


3.2.3 Directory Moves/Renames


dir_id MUST remain stable under rename/move.

.dir_id MUST move with the directory as part of the directory contents.

Registry entries MUST be updated to reflect the new path, but dir_id MUST not change.


3.3 File Identity


3.3.1 File ID Assignment Rule


If file is in zone=governed:
If filename already contains a valid ID prefix → parse and use it as file_id

Else allocate a new file_id from the global allocator and apply filename prefixing (rename)


3.3.2 Filename Prefixing Format


Standard format:
<ID_PREFIX><FILE_ID>__<original_filename>

Where:
FILE_ID is the global allocator ID

__ is the canonical separator


3.3.3 Python Special Prefix Rule


If file is Python (.py) and governed:
ID_PREFIX = "P_"

If file is not Python:
ID_PREFIX = "" (empty) unless explicitly extended by a separate Prefix Policy


Examples:


Python: P_01999000042260124506__validator.py

Non-Python: 01999000042260124522__notes.md


3.3.4 File Moves/Renames


file_id MUST remain stable under move/rename.

The ID prefix MUST remain in the filename in governed zone.

If a user renames a file and removes the prefix in governed zone, this is a blocking gate failure and must be corrected (rename back or re-register under a new id based on policy; default is restore original id).



Staging Behavior (Root-Level Looseness)


4.1 Default Non-Governance in Staging


In zone=staging (depth 0):


IDs are optional.

.dir_id is not required.

Files are not required to be renamed.

Registry updates are not required and SHOULD be skipped by default.


4.2 Explicit Promotion


The system MUST support an explicit action promote_to_governed(path) which:
moves/creates the target under depth ≥ 1 (or confirms it already is)

enforces directory .dir_id

enforces file ID prefixing for files under that governed subtree

writes/updates registry entries deterministically



Exclusion Policy


5.1 Optional Exclusions


The system MAY define explicit excluded subtrees under governed zone (examples: .git/, .venv/, __pycache__/, build outputs).


Exclusions MUST be defined in a machine-readable exclusion_policy contract.

Excluded paths are treated as zone=excluded.

Excluded paths MUST NOT require IDs and SHOULD NOT be registered.


5.2 Exclusion Overrides


A path under an excluded subtree can be force-governed only via explicit override rules (rare; default is no overrides).



Global Allocator Policy


6.1 Storage Location


Allocator state MUST live outside the project root so moving/copying projects does not reset counters.


6.2 Atomicity


Allocation MUST be atomic under concurrency.

If atomic allocation cannot be guaranteed, the allocator is considered invalid and execution MUST fail.


6.3 Allocation Record (Audit)


Every allocation MUST append an immutable log record containing at minimum:


issued_id

issued_at_utc

entity_kind (file|directory)

project_root_id (stable identifier for root)

relative_path_at_allocation

reason (register|promote|mkdir_governed|import|repair)

allocator_version


6.4 Non-Reuse


Issued IDs MUST NEVER be reused, even if files/directories are deleted.



Registry Policy


7.1 Entity Kinds


The registry MUST support at least:


entity_kind=file

entity_kind=directory


7.2 Required Registry Presence


For zone=governed:
Every directory MUST have a directory record keyed by dir_id

Every file MUST have a file record keyed by file_id


7.3 Deterministic Column Population


All non-identity columns MUST be populated according to:
column dictionary requiredness rules

derivations contract

write policy rules

Manual population is disallowed where derivation exists (unless write policy explicitly permits override with evidence).


7.4 Patch-Only Mutation


Registry updates MUST be applied via patch operations (RFC-6902 semantics).

Direct rewrite of the full registry without patch provenance is disallowed.



Unified Runtime Trigger Policy


8.1 Triggers


The system MUST recognize trigger kinds:


DIR_CREATED, DIR_MOVED, DIR_DELETED

FILE_CREATED, FILE_MOVED, FILE_MODIFIED, FILE_DELETED

PROMOTE_REQUESTED (explicit action)


8.2 Zone-Based Routing (Hard Rule)


If zone=staging: do nothing by default (no IDs required, no registry updates) unless PROMOTE_REQUESTED or explicit “register” is requested.

If zone=governed: enforce identity + registry.



Gate Catalog (Exact Required Gates)


9.1 Directory Gates (Governed Zone)


DIR-G1 (blocking): Every governed directory has a .dir_id file.

DIR-G2 (blocking): .dir_id parses and contains exactly one valid dir_id.

DIR-G3 (blocking): dir_id is unique in registry scope.

DIR-G4 (blocking): Registry contains a directory record for each governed directory.

DIR-G5 (blocking): Directory record required fields present per column dictionary for entity_kind=directory.

DIR-G6 (optional): Semantic key coverage thresholds met (only if semantic keys are enabled).


9.2 File Gates (Governed Zone)


FILE-G1 (blocking): Every governed file has a valid file_id in its filename prefix.

FILE-G2 (blocking): Python governed files MUST have P_ prefix before file_id.

FILE-G3 (blocking): file_id unique in registry scope.

FILE-G4 (blocking): Registry contains a file record for each governed file.

FILE-G5 (blocking): File record required fields present per column dictionary.

FILE-G6 (optional): Derived fields match derivations contract (enforce determinism checks).


9.3 Cross-Cutting Gates


X-G1 (blocking): Staging zone is not auto-registered by default.

X-G2 (blocking): Registry changes are patch-only.

X-G3 (blocking): Allocator state accessible and atomicity guaranteed.

X-G4 (blocking): Any identity repair actions produce evidence artifacts and audit log entries.



Migration Policy (from hash-derived dir_id to allocator-issued)


10.1 Detection


If .dir_id exists and matches legacy hash-derived pattern, treat as legacy_dir_id.


10.2 Migration Rule (Default)


For each governed directory with legacy_dir_id:
allocate a new allocator-issued dir_id

write it into .dir_id (replacing legacy content)

record the legacy value in registry as dir_id_legacy (or prior_ids[])

emit migration evidence

update registry references to use new dir_id


10.3 Compatibility Window (Optional)


During a defined window, allow both dir_id and dir_id_legacy to resolve references, but enforce new writes only use dir_id.



Non-Negotiable Invariants (Summary)


One global allocator for both file and directory IDs.

Root depth 0 is staging; depth ≥ 1 is governed.

Every governed directory has .dir_id with allocator-issued dir_id.

Every governed file has allocator-issued file_id in filename; Python adds P_.

Registry is updated only for governed zone by default.

Registry mutation is patch-only.

Gates block violations; fixes require evidence + audit logs.



If you want this to be “exact policy” in the same style as your governance artifacts, the next step is to convert the above into:


a WRITE_POLICY section (what fields can be written/overwritten)

a DERIVATIONS section (exact derived field rules)

a GATE_CATALOG block (machine-enforceable gate metadata)

an EXCLUSION_POLICY block (pattern list + precedence)

Unified File+Directory ID Package v1.0


Fully automated, end-to-end implementation plan (compatible with current File ID system)


This document describes how to build a single, portable ID subsystem that governs both files and directories under a user-specified project_root, while preserving your current File ID ingest behavior (20-digit prefixes, allocator, registry ingestion, deterministic derivations) and extending it to directories using .dir_id anchors and the same global allocator.



Compatibility constraints (non-negotiable)



1.1 Must remain compatible with current File ID runtime


Your current system already supports:


ingest of observed filesystem items into a registry using a contract-driven header selection pipeline

ID-prefixed vs unprefixed file handling

an allocator with locking and controlled fallback

deterministic derivations + patch-only registry mutation


We do not replace this. We extend it to directories by:


adding entity_kind=directory support to the same ingest envelope

adding a directory identity resolution step using .dir_id

using the same global allocator sequence for dir_id and file_id


1.2 Directory identity must be allocator-issued (not hash-derived)


You currently have a DIR_ID guide that describes hash-derived DIR-{slug}-{hash}. That conflicts with your “one counter for files+dirs” requirement.


Decision for this implementation: directories use allocator-issued IDs stored in .dir_id.
Hash-derived validation gates are replaced with presence/format/uniqueness gates.


1.3 Governance boundary rule (staging vs governed)


depth = 0 (project root): staging (IDs optional; registry skipped by default)

depth >= 1: governed (dir IDs mandatory; file IDs mandatory)


This is the lever that prevents “temporary file clutter” from entering SSOT.



System architecture



2.1 Components


Global Allocator Store (portable across projects)

Watcher + Scanner (detect events + reconcile drift)

Unified Ingest Engine (contract-driven; supports file + directory)

Identity Resolvers
File resolver: parse prefix or allocate; rename

Dir resolver: read .dir_id or allocate; write .dir_id

Registry Writer (patch-only; same for both)

Gate Runner (same framework; adds directory gates)

Evidence Writer (same envelope; directory-specific evidence kinds)

Migration Tooling (optional, for converting legacy hash-derived .dir_id)


2.2 Data flow (single runtime)


Trigger → Ingest Envelope → Zone Routing → Identity Resolution → Populate (contracts) → Gates → Patch Commit → Verify


entity_kind and zone control behavior; there is no separate dir system.



Files and directories: identity artifacts



3.1 File identity (unchanged, extended for governed enforcement)


A governed file must have a 20-digit ID in its filename prefix.

If it is .py, filename must include P_ immediately before the 20-digit ID.

If a governed file is unprefixed, the system allocates an ID and renames it.


Canonical filename pattern


Python: P_<FILE_ID>__<human_name>.py

Other: <FILE_ID>__<human_name>.<ext>


3.2 Directory identity (new behavior)


Every governed directory must contain a .dir_id file.

.dir_id stores allocator-issued dir_id plus metadata.


.dir_id file format (recommended JSON)


{
  "dir_id": "01999000042260124550",
  "allocator_version": "1.0.0",
  "allocated_at_utc": "2026-02-13T06:12:00Z",
  "project_root_id": "ROOT-<stable>",
  "relative_path": "src/module_a"
}


Notes:


dir_id is a 20-digit ID from the same allocator sequence as files.

relative_path is informational; dir_id does not change on move/rename.



Global allocator store (the key to cross-project uniqueness)



4.1 Storage location requirement


Allocator state must live outside the project root so copying/moving projects does not reset IDs.


Recommended default:


Windows: %APPDATA%\GovReg\IdAllocator\

Cross-platform: ~/.gov_reg/id_allocator/


4.2 Required files in allocator store


allocator_state.json
Holds: last_issued_id, allocator_version, counter_max, etc.

allocation_log.ndjson (append-only)
Every allocation event appended as immutable record.

allocator.lock (or OS-native lock primitive)


4.3 Atomic allocation contract


Allocator must guarantee:


exclusive lock acquisition

no duplicate issuance under concurrency

crash-safe state update (write temp + atomic rename)


4.4 Allocation log record (minimum)


{
  "issued_id": "01999000042260124550",
